text: The left lateral segment of the liver is fully mobilized, with the falciform ligament visibly divided.
The surgeon elevates the left liver and divides the gastrohepatic ligament, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon visually identifies the replaced left hepatic artery in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the ligamentum venosum, revealing the inferior vena cava and the entry point of the replaced left hepatic artery into the liver.

The surgeon dissects the left hepatic vein circumferentially, noting that in this patient, the left and middle hepatic veins drain separately into the inferior vena cava, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon performs circumferential dissection of the left hepatic vein at its drainage point into the inferior vena cava, with clear visualization of the anatomical structures.
After completing the isolation of the hepatic outflow, the surgeon focuses on the umbilical fissure, where the portal vein is visible coursing over the caudate lobe before entering the left liver.
The surgeon exposes and carefully dissects the portal vein, freeing it from surrounding tissues near the umbilical fissure.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the replaced left hepatic artery and ligates it using laparoscopic hemlock clips, which are placed through the assistant port.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to elevate the liver after ligating and dividing the replaced left hepatic artery, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The portal vein is clearly visualized and circumferentially dissected free, with surrounding tissues carefully separated to expose the vessel.
The surgeon uses ultrasound imaging to identify the tumor and confirm the absence of disease in the remnant liver.
The surgeon uses imaging to verify the vascular anatomy before proceeding to divide the portal vein, ensuring accurate identification of the vessel's structure.
During the parenchymal transection, the surgeon frequently uses ultrasound to verify that the margins are adequate, ensuring precise separation of liver tissue.
The surgeon uses a drop-in ultrasound probe inserted through the assistant port to visualize internal structures during the procedure.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to place hemlock clips on the left portal vein after confirming the vascular anatomy with ultrasound.
The surgeon ligates the left hepatic vein using robotic hemlock clips, similar to the previous ligation of the left portal vein.
The surgeon divides the left portal vein using robotic hemlock clips, while the hepatic vein remains undivided due to limited space for clip placement.
The anterior surface of the liver shows a visible line of demarcation after the inflow and outflow vessels have been ligated.

The surgeon administers a peripheral injection of five milligrams of indocyanine green to the patient. Fluorescence imaging is then utilized to visually verify the border between the perfused and unperfused areas of the liver.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to mark the liver surface, creating a visible guide for the upcoming transection.
Vessel loops are sutured into the liver on either side of the line of transection to prepare for the parenchymal transection, with the demarcation line visible on the liver surface.
The surgeon brings the vessel loops out of the abdomen percutaneously to apply lateral traction, aiding in the stabilization of the liver during the parenchymal transection.
The surgeon divides the liver parenchyma using bipolar energy, manipulating a Maryland dissector in one hand and a Grasper in the other, following the marked line of demarcation on the liver surface.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to carefully shape the transection line on the liver, ensuring adequate margins for the procedure.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the vessels and bile ducts, securing them with clips to prevent bleeding and bile leakage.
The surgeon staples large veins and portal structures, securing them within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bipolar grasper in the left hand to control oozing from the cut surface of the liver, demonstrating precise hemostatic technique.
The surgeon maintains retraction to ensure clear visualization, allowing the liver parenchyma to separate easily while preventing tearing of the vessels.
The surgeon uses percutaneous rubber band retractors and the fourth robotic arm to maintain optimal retraction of the liver, ensuring clear visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a hanging maneuver using umbilical tape to achieve optimal retraction and visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the hanging maneuver to facilitate retraction, employing umbilical tape to maintain optimal exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs the hanging maneuver using umbilical tape to control venous bleeding during the final portion of the parenchymal transection.
Following the completion of the parenchymal transection, the surgeon prepares to extend the umbilical incision for specimen removal, choosing between a fan and steel incision for larger specimens.
The surgeon completes the parenchymal transection, with the left hepatic vein already ligated extrahepatically, eliminating the need for further manipulation of this vessel.
The interparenchymal portion of the vein is visible within the surgical field, showing its position relative to the surrounding liver tissue.
The surgeon uses a surgical stapler to divide the remaining portion of the liver parenchyma and the hepatic vein.
The surgeon employs the hanging maneuver to facilitate the transection of the liver parenchyma, providing a clear view of the interparenchymal structures.
The final view reveals the completed parenchymal transection, with the surgical field showing the separated liver tissue and any remaining anatomical structures.
